content.text: Why Handoffs Fail

An IC handoff is one of the highest-risk moments in a long-running incident. The incoming IC lacks the context that the outgoing IC has accumulated over the last 2–4 hours: the hypotheses that were explored and discarded, the provider contacts that were already engaged, the stakeholders who received specific commitments, and the mitigation steps that are in-progress but not yet reflected in the channel history. A handoff that consists of 'you're IC now, check the channel' transfers the role without the context, and the new IC will spend 20–30 minutes reconstructing what the outgoing IC already knows.

De-escalation failures are symmetric: an IC who holds a SEV 1 open after user impact has ended creates false urgency, burns responder time, and skews the incident metrics that the team uses to trend reliability. But premature de-escalation — closing a SEV 1 while the root cause is still unknown and the mitigation is not yet proven stable — is worse, because the incident reopens and has to be re-established.

attrs.alt: IC handoff and de-escalation flow: handoff trigger check every 60 min, 4-step handoff process (notify, brief, post message, incoming IC acks), de-escalation check (user impact gone + root cause understood + mitigation stable 30 min), resolution actions. Timing rules by severity.

content.text: When to Hand Off
The handoff decision is the IC's to make, but the roster structure constrains it. At minimum, a handoff is required at:
SEV 1: the IC has been active for more than 2 hours with no resolution in sight. Long incidents erode IC judgment through fatigue and context bias.
Any severity: the IC's primary on-call shift ends. The on-call engineer cannot be both primary responder and IC on the next shift if they need rest.
Any severity: the IC is needed for a higher-priority incident that fires during the current one. An IC cannot manage two active incidents simultaneously — one must be handed off.
SEV 1 or 2: the IC has been active for 4 hours continuously, regardless of progress. This is a hard cap.
Discretionary handoff triggers (IC judgment call):
The IC feels their decision quality degrading — they are starting to anchor on a single hypothesis, missing obvious questions, or forgetting what has been tried
The incoming IC is a specialist more suited to the current phase (e.g., the incident has shifted from availability to a quality/safety track that requires a different expertise background)
The incident has shifted across multiple time zones and the incoming IC is geographically closer to the affected users or the provider's support team
The Four-Step Handoff Protocol
A structured handoff takes 10–15 minutes and has four steps. Never skip the verbal briefing step — async channel handoffs lose the nuance that shapes the incoming IC's first decisions.
Step 1: Notify the incoming IC

Page the backup IC (or next-in-rotation if the backup is unavailable) with the explicit message that a handoff is being initiated. Include in the page: the incident ID, current severity, time of the handoff call, and the outgoing IC's availability for follow-up questions post-handoff. Do not just transfer the PagerDuty acknowledgment — the incoming IC needs context before they pick up the call.

# Handoff initiation page (send to incoming IC)
@dave — handoff initiated for INC-2026-0701-002 (SEV 1, Safety)
T+2h15m. Outgoing IC: @alice.
Handoff call: join #inc-20260701-002 video bridge now.
alice available on Slack for 30 min post-handoff for questions.
Step 2: The 5-minute verbal briefing

The briefing happens on the video bridge with both ICs present. Use the SBAR format: Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation. The outgoing IC speaks; the incoming IC listens and asks at most two clarifying questions during the brief (save longer questions for after the handoff message is posted).

# SBAR Handoff Brief (outgoing IC speaks, ~5 min)

**Situation:**
INC-2026-0701-002, SEV 1 Safety. Declared 07:20 UTC, now T+2h15m.
A safety classifier flagged harmful outputs from the recommendations feature
affecting approximately 340 users before the feature was disabled at 07:28 UTC.
Feature remains disabled.

**Background:**
Root cause: a prompt template update deployed at 07:10 UTC removed the safety
framing instructions. The classifier caught it at 07:20. The deploy was rolled
back at 07:32 but we have not yet re-enabled the feature pending safety review.
Legal was notified at 07:48 UTC (T+28 min). Sarah Kim (legal) confirmed no
regulatory notification required for this scope — written confirmation in
#inc-20260701-002 at 08:15 UTC.

**Assessment:**
Technical mitigation is complete (rollback, feature disabled). The open question
is: does the safety team need to run a full audit of the affected outputs before
re-enable, or is a sample audit sufficient? That decision is pending a call
with Maya (safety lead) at 10:00 UTC.

**Recommendation:**
Incoming IC's main job: attend the 10:00 UTC safety call, get the audit
decision, and either schedule re-enable or escalate if the audit reveals more
affected outputs than the current estimate of 340. Keep the feature disabled
until safety sign-off. 30-min status updates to the channel until resolved.
Step 3: Post the handoff message to the war room channel

Immediately after the verbal brief, the outgoing IC posts the handoff record to the incident channel. This is the public, permanent record of the transfer of command. The incoming IC reads it back to confirm accuracy before acknowledging.

# Handoff Record (post to #inc-20260701-002)

**IC HANDOFF — 09:35 UTC**

**Outgoing IC:** @alice (available on Slack until 10:05 UTC for questions)
**Incoming IC:** @dave (taking command now)

**Current status:** MITIGATING — feature disabled, rollback complete
**Open items:**
1. Safety team audit decision (10:00 UTC call with @maya)
2. User notification decision (pending legal clearance, Sarah Kim to advise)
3. Re-enable timeline unknown until audit complete

**What is NOT open:**
- Root cause identified: prompt template change at 07:10 UTC
- Provider not involved
- Legal confirmed no regulatory notification required (08:15 UTC message)

**Commitments made:**
- Status update to VP Engineering at 10:30 UTC (Alice gave this commitment)
- Next channel status update at 10:05 UTC

**Incoming IC acknowledged:** (dave to reply with "ACK" to this message)
Step 4: Incoming IC acknowledges in the channel

The incoming IC replies to the handoff message with a brief acknowledgment. This timestamp is the official moment of command transfer. If the incoming IC has concerns about the handoff record (missing information, factual errors), they raise them before acknowledging — not after.

# Incoming IC acknowledgment (dave replies to handoff record)

ACK — @dave took IC command at 09:37 UTC.
Confirmed: attending 10:00 UTC safety call, 10:05 status update, 10:30 VP update.
@alice — one question: did you discuss re-enable criteria with maya yet
or is that the purpose of the 10:00 call?

# alice replies:
Purpose of the 10:00 call. No pre-alignment yet. She knows the scope is ~340 users.
De-Escalation: Downgrading Severity

De-escalation is the process of reducing the severity of an active incident when the situation has improved. It is distinct from resolution — an incident can be de-escalated from SEV 1 to SEV 2 while still active. The IC makes the de-escalation decision; the on-call manager must be notified for SEV 1 de-escalations.
De-Escalation Criteria
All three conditions must be true before de-escalating:
User impact has ended
— the metric that originally defined the incident (error rate, safety flag rate, cost explosion) is back within normal bounds and has been stable for at least 30 minutes
Root cause is understood
— the team can articulate what caused the incident. 'We don't know yet' blocks de-escalation. Root cause uncertainty means the problem could return.
Mitigation is stable
— the fix that resolved user impact (rollback, fallback, feature disable) is in place and has been running for at least 30 minutes without signs of re-degradation

Exception: a safety incident cannot be de-escalated below SEV 1 until the safety team has reviewed the affected outputs and confirmed no further user harm is possible. Technical mitigation alone is insufficient for safety de-escalation.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

@dataclass
class DeEscalationCheck:
    user_impact_ended_at: datetime | None    # None = still impacting
    root_cause_known: bool
    mitigation_stable_since: datetime | None  # None = not yet stable
    is_safety_incident: bool
    safety_team_signed_off: bool

def can_de_escalate(check: DeEscalationCheck, now: datetime | None = None) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    now = now or datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    stability_window = timedelta(minutes=30)

    if check.user_impact_ended_at is None:
        return False, "User impact is still ongoing"

    if not check.root_cause_known:
        return False, "Root cause is not yet understood — de-escalation blocked"

    if check.mitigation_stable_since is None:
        return False, "Mitigation not yet confirmed stable"

    stable_duration = now - check.mitigation_stable_since
    if stable_duration < stability_window:
        remaining = stability_window - stable_duration
        return False, f"Mitigation stable for {stable_duration.seconds // 60} min; wait {remaining.seconds // 60} more min"

    if check.is_safety_incident and not check.safety_team_signed_off:
        return False, "Safety incident: requires safety team sign-off before de-escalation"

    return True, "All de-escalation criteria met"

# Example: checking a safety incident at T+1h30m
check = DeEscalationCheck(
    user_impact_ended_at=datetime(2026, 7, 1, 8, 30, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
    root_cause_known=True,
    mitigation_stable_since=datetime(2026, 7, 1, 8, 35, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
    is_safety_incident=True,
    safety_team_signed_off=False,  # still pending
)
ok, reason = can_de_escalate(check, now=datetime(2026, 7, 1, 9, 20, tzinfo=timezone.utc))
print(ok, reason)
# False, "Safety incident: requires safety team sign-off before de-escalation"
De-Escalation Message
When de-escalating, post the downgrade to the incident channel and update the severity in the incident tracking system.

# De-escalation message (post to incident channel)

**SEVERITY DOWNGRADE — 09:55 UTC**

INC-2026-0701-002 downgraded from **SEV 1 → SEV 2**.

**Reason:**
- User impact ended at 07:28 UTC (feature disabled)
- Root cause confirmed: prompt template change at 07:10 UTC, rolled back at 07:32 UTC
- Mitigation (feature disabled + rollback) stable since 07:35 UTC (2h20m)
- Safety team audit still in progress (not yet signed off for re-enable)

**What changes with SEV 2:**
- Status update cadence: every 30 min (was 15 min)
- VP Engineering loop: removed (notified of downgrade via email)
- Legal loop: maintained (safety audit still active)

**Still open:**
- Safety team audit (10:00 UTC call)
- Re-enable decision and timeline

On-call manager @carol notified of downgrade.
Resolving the Incident

Resolution is the final state change. An incident is resolved when the service is back to normal operation and no further monitoring or action is required from the incident response team. Resolution does not mean the postmortem is complete — it means the incident response is over and the team can return to normal operations.
Resolution Criteria
All four conditions must hold:
The triggering metric (error rate, safety flag rate, cost) has been at normal levels for at least 30 minutes
Root cause is documented in the incident record
No further monitoring is needed beyond normal production monitoring — the on-call engineer is not doing incident-mode monitoring
All open action items from the incident have been logged as tickets (not necessarily resolved, but logged)

# Resolution message (post to incident channel)

**INCIDENT RESOLVED — 11:15 UTC**

INC-2026-0701-002 — **RESOLVED**

**Duration:** 07:20 UTC → 11:15 UTC (T+3h55m)
**Final severity:** SEV 2 (downgraded from SEV 1 at 09:55 UTC)
**Root cause:** Prompt template update at 07:10 UTC removed safety framing.
Feature re-enabled with corrected template at 11:10 UTC after safety team
sign-off (Maya Chen, 11:05 UTC).

**User impact:**
- 340 users received potentially unsafe outputs between 07:10–07:28 UTC
- Feature was disabled for 3h42m

**Action items logged:**
- PROD-4821: Add safety framing to prompt template CI validation
- PROD-4822: Implement staging environment check for safety classifier regression
- PROD-4823: User notification letter (legal to draft)

**Postmortem:** https://docs.internal/postmortems/INC-2026-0701-002
First draft due: 2026-07-03 (48h SLA)

IC: @dave
Status page updated. #inc-20260701-002 archived.
Status Page Update on Resolution
The public status page update at resolution is the last external communication the IC is responsible for. It must be factual, brief, and not assign blame to a team, vendor, or individual.

# Status page post — Resolved

**Resolved: GenAI Recommendation Service Disruption**
July 1, 2026 — 11:15 UTC

The recommendation service has been fully restored as of 11:10 UTC.

Between 07:10 UTC and 07:28 UTC, a subset of users received responses from
our recommendations feature that did not meet our quality standards. We
disabled the feature at 07:28 UTC and have since resolved the underlying
configuration issue. The feature was re-enabled at 11:10 UTC.

We are conducting a full review of this incident and will publish a summary
of our findings and corrective actions within 5 business days.

We apologize for the disruption and for any impact this caused to our users.
Handoff Anti-Patterns
Anti-Pattern
What Goes Wrong
Correct Practice
Async-only handoff
Incoming IC reads the channel for 20 min to reconstruct context; misses nuances not captured in messages
Always do a verbal briefing, even if brief. Async message supplements the call, does not replace it.
No acknowledgment
Unclear who is IC; both ICs continue making decisions; conflicting status updates posted
Incoming IC must explicitly acknowledge in the channel before outgoing IC is released
Handoff mid-action
Outgoing IC transfers command while a mitigation deploy is in-flight; incoming IC does not know what to watch for
Wait for the current action to complete or reach a stable checkpoint before handing off
Premature resolution
Incident closed while root cause unknown; same issue recurs 2 hours later; restarting the response costs 30 min
Resolution requires root cause documented. 'We think it was X' is not a root cause.
Safety de-escalation without sign-off
IC downgrades safety SEV 1 to SEV 2 after feature disable; safety team had not completed audit; re-enable decision gets lost
Safety incidents need explicit safety team sign-off. Feature disable is mitigation, not resolution.
Summary

Handoff and de-escalation are the operational bookends of a long-running incident. A good handoff requires a verbal briefing using SBAR format, a written handoff record in the war room channel, and an explicit acknowledgment by the incoming IC. De-escalation requires three conditions to hold simultaneously: user impact ended, root cause understood, and mitigation stable for 30 minutes — with an additional safety team sign-off gate for safety incidents. Resolution requires four conditions, including all action items logged as tickets. The most common failures are async-only handoffs that lose context, premature resolution while root cause is unknown, and safety de-escalation without explicit sign-off from the safety team.
